text: The surgeon uses robotic instruments to suspend the uterus, moving it out of the surgical field to provide better access for the procedure.
The surgeon begins a medial dissection over the sacral promontory to access the retro-rectal space, using robotic instruments.
The surgical field reveals clear visibility of the pelvic nerves, indicating the patient's favorable anatomical presentation.
The surgeon carefully preserves the visible nerves by sweeping them posteriorly off the posterior plane of the mesorectum during the dissection.
The surgeon performs a full posterior mobilization, right lateral mobilization, and anterior mobilization of the rectum, while leaving the left side untouched.
The surgeon adjusts the camera position to arm two, placing it more towards the midline to improve the angle for deeper dissection. The setup now includes two right arms and one left arm.
The surgeon continues the dissection of the mesorectum, maintaining careful separation of tissue planes while preserving nerves by sweeping them posteriorly.
The surgeon visualizes the deep anterior cul-de-sac and begins to define the dissection plane in the rectovaginal septum.
The surgeon carefully dissects in the distal region, avoiding the use of cautery to prevent injury to the vagina and rectum.
The surgeon performs a digital rectal exam to assess the depth of the dissection, confirming proximity to the perineal body, and proceeds to complete the right-sided dissection.
The surgeon is operating in the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane, closely adjacent to the rectal wall on the elevator floor, focusing on mobilizing the tissue as far as possible.
The surgeon reaches the deepest part of the dissection, identifying the anococcygeal ligament, while carefully maneuvering around the rectal wall.
The surgeon uses arm number three of the laparoscopic setup to place pexy sutures, securing the tissue in the surgical field.

The surgeon uses bipolar cautery over the bone before placing pexy sutures, carefully pushing the nerve aside. Dissection arm number one pulls the right lateral stalk into position, while the surgeon assesses the redundancy and elevates the distal rectum using a 2-0 silk suture.
The surgeon takes a bite of the periosteum, followed by a precise bite through the lateral stalk, extending right up to the wall of the rectum.
The surgeon takes a parallel bite through the tissue for additional security, ensuring precise placement of the suture.
The surgeon ties down the pexy sutures, securing the previously manipulated tissue in place.
The surgeon places the second pexy suture, ensuring the rectum is taut and under tension.
The surgeon passes a suture through the periosteum, up through the lateral stalk, and up to the rectal wall, then back down through the same path before tying it down to secure the tissue.
The surgeon closes the peritoneal opening in the right lateral rectal dissection using a V-lock suture, securing the tissue layers together.
The surgeon ensures the ureter is identified and protected before completing the suturing process, followed by drying the surgical field.
The surgeon inspects the rectum, which is held on tension by the pexy suture, ensuring there is no folding or kinking. The uterus is lowered, and any bleeding from the suture is addressed.
